name: rosemary olive oil cake
instructions:
INGREDIENTS:

Dry:
- 3/4 cup / 80g spelt flour (or whole wheat pastry flour)
- 1 1/2 cups / 210 g all-purpose flour
- 3/4 cup / 115g sugar
- 1 1/2 tsp baking powder
- 3/4 tsp kosher salt

Wet:
- 3 whole eggs
- 1 cup / 240 ml olive oil
- 3/4 cup / 180 ml whole milk
- 1 1/2 tbsp fresh rosemary, finely chopped

Mix-in and topping:
- 5 oz / 140 g bittersweet chocolate (~70% cacao), chopped into ~1/2-inch pieces
- 2 tbsp sugar (for top crust)
- olive oil (for greasing the pan)

PROCEDURE:

1. Preheat oven to 350°F / 175°C. Rub a 9.5-inch (24 cm) fluted tart pan (or equivalent) with olive oil, or line with parchment.
2. Sift the dry ingredients into a large bowl. Pour any grain bits left in the sifter back into the bowl.
3. In a separate large bowl, whisk the eggs thoroughly. Add olive oil, milk, and chopped rosemary; whisk again.
4. Fold wet into dry with a spatula — gently, just until combined. Don't overmix.
5. Stir in 2/3 of the chopped chocolate.
6. Pour batter into the prepared pan, spreading evenly and smoothing the top. Sprinkle the remaining 1/3 of chocolate on top. Run a fork along the length so the batter envelops the chunks slightly. Sprinkle with the 2 tbsp topping sugar.
7. Bake ~40 minutes (up to 50 min if using a deeper pan) until the top is domed, golden brown, and a skewer inserted in the center comes out clean.
8. OPTIONAL: for more color and a crunchier caramelized top, finish under the broiler for ~1 min at the end. Don't walk away.
9. Cool in pan. Can be eaten warm or cool. Wrapped tightly, keeps 2-3 days.
notes:
Kim Boyce's Rosemary Olive Oil Cake, via 101 Cookbooks. A rustic olive oil cake flecked with rosemary and dotted with chopped bittersweet chocolate. Serves 8-12.

WHY OLIVE OIL OVER BUTTER: olive oil cakes have an even crumb, stay moist longer (butter has ~15% water that develops gluten strands during mixing; olive oil is fat-only), and don't require room-temperature-butter prep. Good weekday cake as a result — 10 minutes to get it in the oven.

ROSEMARY IS THE WILDCARD — SUBTLE, NOT OVERPOWERING: the rosemary heats up in the oven as the cake bakes and permeates it gradually. Result is savory-herbal aromatics that read as accent, not headliner. Chop it fine.

USE A FLAVORFUL OLIVE OIL: no need for a specialty oil, but if you have a peppery, grassy EVOO on hand, the cake will be that much better. The olive oil is doing structural flavor work here.

REGULAR SUGAR, NOT BROWN / MUSCOVADO: the interplay of rosemary + chocolate + olive oil is already doing plenty; less-refined sugars don't help this cake. Stick with white granulated.

CHOP A CHOCOLATE BAR — DON'T USE CHOCOLATE CHIPS: uniform chips give you a uniform result. Chopping a bar produces big ~1/2-inch chunks along with flecks and shavings, and the mix of sizes is what makes each bite interesting. Bittersweet ~70% cacao is the target.

SPELT FLOUR OR WHOLE WHEAT PASTRY FLOUR (INTERCHANGEABLE HERE): either works for the 3/4 cup whole-grain slot. Spelt has slightly nuttier character; whole wheat pastry is more common in american pantries. Don't sub all all-purpose — the whole-grain fraction is doing textural work.

PAN SIZE — FLUTED TART PAN CANONICAL BUT FLEXIBLE: 9.5-inch fluted tart pan is Kim's original. 9-inch round works (don't fill more than 2/3). Loaf pan or vintage baton pan work too but the bake time extends (up to 50 min). Muffin tins also fine — reduce bake time.

DON'T OVERMIX: fold wet into dry just until combined. Overmixing develops gluten and toughens the cake. This is a fold-and-stop batter.

OPTIONAL BROILER FINISH: 1 min under the broiler at the end caramelizes the topping sugar and adds a crunchy sugar-and-chocolate crust. Watch it constantly — a broiler moment can burn to black in seconds.

GREAT FOR PICNICS, ROAD TRIPS, LUNCHBOXES: cake travels well, stays moist for 2-3 days wrapped tight, and reads as an understated grown-up dessert.

COMMENT-SECTION VARIATIONS (worth noting):
- Half the batter with chocolate, half without: some commenters find the chocolate can overpower the delicate rosemary + olive oil register.
- Oat flour substituted for spelt: reported to work well.
- Yogurt (full fat) for milk, coconut sugar for regular sugar: untested but plausible substitutions per commenters.
